questions.question: What is a function overload in TypeScript and what problem does it solve?

choices.a: It allows redefining a function from a parent class
choices.b: It provides multiple type signatures for one implementation, letting callers get precise return types based on argument types
choices.c: It lets you define multiple implementations with the same name at runtime
choices.d: It is the same as optional parameters

questions.question: What does a `this` parameter in a TypeScript function do?

choices.a: It is a hidden extra parameter passed at runtime
choices.b: It declares the expected type of `this` inside the function; it is erased at compile time
choices.c: It is only valid inside class methods
choices.d: It binds the function to a specific object like `.bind()`

questions.question: What is the difference between a parameter being optional (`param?: T`) vs having a default value (`param = value`)?

choices.a: They are identical â€” only stylistic difference
choices.b: Optional params have type `T | undefined`; default params have type `T` (TypeScript considers them always provided when called without that argument)
choices.c: Default params are not part of TypeScript â€” they are only JavaScript
choices.d: Optional params must always come last

questions.explanation:
â€˘ `param?: T` â†’ the parameter has type `T | undefined`. Inside the function, you must handle the case where it is `undefined`.

â€˘ `param: T = defaultValue` â†’ TypeScript treats the parameter as TYPE `T` inside the function body (the default handles the undefined case). Callers can omit it or pass `undefined`, and the default kicks in.

Note: `param?: T` and `param: T | undefined` are almost the same but `?` also means you can OMIT the argument entirely. `T | undefined` without `?` means you must explicitly pass `undefined`.

Default parameters can appear anywhere in the list (except a required param cannot come after them in a meaningful way). Optional params (`?`) cannot be followed by required params.

questions.question: What is the type of the function `(...args: number[]) => number`?

choices.b: `(...args: number[]) => number` â€” a precise function type with typed rest params

questions.question: What is `ReturnType<T>` and how do you extract the return type of an async function?

choices.a: It returns the type of the first parameter of T
choices.b: `ReturnType<T>` gives the return type; for async functions use `Awaited<ReturnType<T>>` to unwrap the Promise
choices.c: `ReturnType<T>` only works with synchronous functions and throws on async ones
choices.d: `ReturnType<T>` gives `Promise<SomeType>` and there is no way to unwrap it

questions.question: What does it mean for a function type to be contravariant in its parameter types?

choices.b: A function expecting a broader type is assignable to a function expecting a narrower type (parameter types flip)
choices.c: Parameter covariance means subtype of parameter = subtype of function
choices.d: TypeScript always enforces strict covariance for parameters

questions.question: When should you use `call signatures` inside an interface instead of a simple function type alias?

choices.a: Never â€” function type aliases are always preferred
choices.b: When the callable also needs additional properties (like a function with a `.cache` property)
choices.c: Only when working with class decorators
choices.d: Call signatures in interfaces are deprecated in modern TypeScript

questions.explanation:
A function type alias like `type F = () => void` only describes a bare callable. If the function object also carries properties, you need a call signature inside an interface:

```typescript
interface Cached {
  (): string;        // callable
  cache: string[];   // property on the function object
  clear(): void;     // method on the function object
}
```

This is used in patterns like memoization, middleware stacks, or jQuery-style APIs where functions carry data. You cannot express this with a simple arrow function type (`() => string` has no room for additional properties).

questions.question: What happens to TypeScript types at runtime?

choices.a: Types are compiled into runtime checks similar to decorators
choices.b: Types are completely erased â€” the emitted JavaScript has no type information
choices.c: Types are embedded in comments in the JS output
choices.d: Types remain as metadata attached to values
